content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
May 10, 2017 
Jonathan W. Smith 
Deputy City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
1017 Middlefield Road 
Redwood City, CA 94063 
Re: 
Your Request for Informal Assistance 
Our File No. 1-17-094 
Dear Mr. Smith: 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Redwood City Councilmember 
Diane Howard regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform 
Act (the "Act"}.1 Please note that we only provide advice under the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. We are not a finder of fact when rendering assistance (/11 re Oglesby 
( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any assistance we provide assumes your facts are complete and 
accurate. Given that your questions are general in nature with limited facts, we treat your request as 
one for informal assistance.2 
QUESTIONS 
I. May Councilmember Howard make, participate in making, or influence upcoming 
decisions pertaining to the specified near-term and long-term amendments to Downtown Precise 
Plan ("DTPP")? 
2. Are the decisions for which the Councilmember has a conflict of interest segmentable 
from other decisions to allow the Councilmember to participate in the segmented decisions? 
FACTS 
The DTPP: Development in the downtown area is governed by the DTPP. The DTPP 
establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 183-acre DTPP area. It sets: 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Infonnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3).) 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 2 
• Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD" Caps) of2,500 net new dwelling 
units; 
• 100,000 square feet of net new retail development; 
• 500,000 square feet of net new commercial office space; and 
• 200 net new lodging rooms. 
For each project, any net new units and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount 
available for other projects under the relevant MAD Cap. 
As of February 13, 2017, after accounting for alJ approved and proposed projects, the 
following development units are left: 
• 179 net new dwelling units; 
• 99,028 square feet of net new retail development; 
• 610 square feet of net new commercial office space; and 
• 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps. 
The Councilmember 's Interests: Councilmember Howard and her spouse own a 4.34358% 
interest in Sequoia Main Street, LLC (the "LLC"). The LLC owns the historic Sequoia Hotel at 800 
Main Street, which is located within the DTPP area. The hotel is a 54-room single-room occupancy 
hotel with a shared bathroom facility. The rooms are rented on a weekly or monthly basis at rates 
lower than those in typical business and tourist hotels. In addition to the hotel rooms, the building 
contains ground floor retail tenants who lease space from the LLC. The retail tenants currently 
include an antique store, a butcher shop, a liquor/grocery store, a nail salon, a book store, and a 
tobacco shop. The hotel provides no automobile or bicycle parking. 
As minority owners in the LLC, Councilmember Howard and her husband receive a 
periodic distribution from the LLC derived from operating profits from the real estate and hotel 
operations, which is comprised of business income, rental income, and interest income. 
Councilmember Howard's investment in the LLC is valued at $2,000 or more, and, during the past 
12 months, she has received distributions from the LLC that equal or exceed $500. 
Potential Upcoming Amendments to the Dow11tow11 Precise Plan. On February 13, 2017, the 
City Council held a study session to review implementation of the DTPP since its initial passage in 
2011 and to discuss future development under the DTPP. In an abundance of caution, 
Councilmember Howard recused herself from the study session. Based on the City Council 
direction at that study session, City staff plans to bring several potential amendments to the City 
Council for its initial consideration. If the City Council elects to proceed with any of the 
amendments, City staff would draft such amendments and bring the amendments to the Planning 
Commission and the City Council for formal consideration. City staff plans to bring the potential 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 3 
amendments to Council in two sets: a first set of potential amendments to be considered in the 
coming months (the "Near-Term Amendments") and a second set of potential amendments to be 
considered over a longer time-horizon (the "Long-Term Amendments"). 
DISCUSSION 
Conflict of Interest Law 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment3 worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
An "indirect" investment or interest in real property is defined as "any investment or interest 
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public 
official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and 
dependent child_ren own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a JO-percent interest or greater." 
(Section 87103.) Because the councilmember has less than IO-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the investments and property owned by the LLC are not interests of the council member. 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his or her 
spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a I 0-percent or greater interest. (Section 
82030(a).) However, because the councilmember has less than I 0-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the clients of the company are not sources of income to Councilmember Howard. (Section 
82030(a).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103( d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
[$470] or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
.:l "Indirect investment" is defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section 
87103.) 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 4 
6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the 
personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth 
the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and subdivision (c) of that 
regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity or real 
property, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests, or 
Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. 
Councilmember Howard is a public official subject to the Act. Assuming her investment in 
the LLC is valued at $2,000 or more, she has an interest in the LLC as a business entity. (Section 
87103.) She also has an interest in the LLC as a source of income assuming she receives a 
distribution that equals or exceeds $500 in the 12 months prior to a governmental decision. (Section 
87103(c).) Thus, she would have a conflict of interest in decision that will have a reasonably 
foreseeable and material financial effect on the LLC. 
Foreseeability 
Explicitly Involved Interests: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether 
an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 1870 I (a) provides: 
"(a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or 
the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any 
license, pennit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and 
includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as 
described in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l )-(6)."4 
None of the decisions explicitly involve the LLC. 
Not Explicitly Involved: In all other cases, Regulation 1870 I (b) provides that if a financial 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is 
reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances 
not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Deemed Material 
The standard to determine materiality for a business entity is set forth in Regulation 
18702.I.5 
4 Regulation I 8702.2(a)( I )-(6) applies lo real property in which the official has a financial interest and 
therefore would nol apply here. 
3 See Regulation I 8702.3(a)(4). Where the source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 refers 
back lo the standard of applied in 18 702.1 for business entities. 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 5 
"(a) The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental 
decision on a business entity in which an official has a financial interest identified 
in Section 87103(a) or (d) is material whenever the business entity: 
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be 
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government 
action concerning the business entity; 
"(2) Offers to make a sale of a service or a product to the official's 
agency: 
"(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency; 
"( 4) Is the named manufacturer in a purchase order of any product 
purchased by the official's agency or the sales provider of any products to the 
official's agency that aggregates to $1,000 or more in any 12-month period; 
"(5) Applies for a pennit, license, grant, tax credit, exception, variance, or 
other entitlement that the official's agency is authorized to issue; 
"(6) Is the subject of any inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the 
regulatory authority of the official's agency; or 
"(7) Is otherwise subject to an action taken by the official's agency, the 
effect of which is directed solely at the business entity in which the official has an 
interest." 
None of the decisions you describe fall under the tests set forth above. 
Material if Changes B11si11ess' Stock or Value 
According to Regulation 18702.1 (b ), for all other decisions, the financial effect is material if 
a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly 
traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity. 
Analysis and Conclusions 
In the Barnhill Advice Letter, No. A-16-144 we analyzed a possible conflict of interest of 
Councilmember Howard's with respect to specific projects. In that letter we noted: 
"[T]he Sequoia Hotel has several retail shops such as the liquor/grocery 
store, nail salon and tobacco shop that undoubtedly rely on walk-up business. By 
adding population to the general area, each of these projects potentially adds to 
any existing customer base already enjoyed by these shops. And it is safe to 
assume that such a potential increase in retail traffic would certainly increase the 
value of the Hotel's retail leases. 


*** 
File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 6 
"Moreover, although it could not be detennined whether typical renters of 
the Hotel could qualify for the new low to very low income housing units, the 
possibility exists that such low income housing units could pose competition to 
the Sequoia Hotel rooms that are typically rented out on a weekly or monthly 
basis. 
"Taking these differences into account, and looking to the applicable 
examples of decisions that might impact the value of a privately-held business 
under Regulation 18702.l(b), we believe a prudent person could find it 
reasonably foreseeable that all three proposed projects will contribute to a change 
in the value of the Councilmember Howard's business interest. Thus, she may not 
participate in the decisions." 
None of your questions involve specific projects, however, if specific projects pending or proposed 
would be affected by these decisions, the councilmember may have a conflict of interest in these 
general decisions based on their impact on the specific projects. Since we have no facts regarding 
the impact of these general decisions specific projects (such as those in the Barnhill letter), we leave 
that analysis to you. 
A. Near-Term Amendments 
1. Architectural Style: The DTPP establishes a list of allowable architectural styles for 
development in the DTPP area. These styles va,y for different parts of the DTPP area: for 
example, contempora,y architecture is allowed 011/y in the area east of Marshall Street. 
City Council may consider an amendment to change the allowed architectural styles 
within the DTPP area. 
Amendments to architectural requirements imposed on the LLC's building could contribute 
to a change in the value of the LLC if it were compelled to comply with them currently or when 
they renovate. Additionally, if the requirements were to apply to the property with respect to future 
use, that may effect the LLC's ability to sell it by reducing the interest among potential buyers. 
Therefore, these decisions do appear to contribute to a change in the value of the LLC and the 
Councilmember would have a conflict of interest. 
2. Pocket Parks and Paseos: The City Council may consider an amendment that would 
encourage the creation of smaller "pocket parks" and/or paseos within the DTPP area. 
3. Pedestrian Access and Amenities: The City Council may consider an amendment that 
would allow and encourage the development of pedestrian amenities within the DTPP 
area, such as wayjinding signage, public art, sidewalk ca/es, benches, lighting, and 
landscaping. 
While parks and and pedestrian amenities around the LLC's building could enhance the 
LLC's business, a policy to encourage (not requiring) parks and pedestrian amenities would not 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 7 
appear to affect the LLC's current operations or future ones. Therefore, it would not appear to 
contribute to a change in the value of the LLC. Obviously, specific decisions concerning a park or 
pedestrian amenities near the LLC's building would require additional analysis. 
4. Height-limitations around Historic Courthouse Square: The City Council may consider 
an amendment that would lower the maximum allowable height of development in the 
vicinity of the City's historic courthouse and courthouse square. 
The Historic Courthouse square is approximately two blocks from the LLC's property. 
Absent unusual specific facts, it would not appear likely that limiting the building height near the 
courthouse square would contribute to a change in the value of LLC. You should contact us as 
specific projects come before the city. 
5. Criteria for Allowing Development that Exceeds DTPP MAD Caps: The MAD Caps limit 
allowable development with in the DTPP Area. City Council may adopt a set of criteria 
establishing when a developer could develop in excess of the MAD Caps. Such criteria 
could include provision of parks, provision of substantial affordable housing, or 
development within a currently under-developed portion of Main Street. 
General criteria that must be met in order to develop outside the MAD caps would not 
appear to contribute to a change in the value of LLC. Decisions on specific waivers from the limits 
may change in the value ofLLC, depending on the facts and circumstances. You should contact us 
for advice where an actual waiver is considered. 
6. Task Force for Future Development: The City Council could create and commission a 
Task Force to develop policy goals and visioning for longer-term development in the 
DTPP area. Such a Task Force could play a role in shaping long-term amendments such 
as those considered below. 
Generally, an official would not have a conflict of interest in decisions to create an 
independent task force or appointment decisions related to task force members.6 For example, the 
councilmember would not have a conflict of interest in setting up a task force to consider the entire 
DTPP. Were the investigation focused on the LLC's property or neighborhood, the answer would 
be different. 
B. Long-Term Amendments 
7. MAD Cap Amendments: The City Council may consider an amendment to increase the 
MAD Caps, generally. Unlike number 5 above, which relates to project-specific 
amendments to the MAD Caps, this decision would entail a plan-wide decision to increase 
some or all of the existing MAD Caps. 
6 Several letters conclude that an appointment decision will not create a conflict for an official in circumstances 
where: (l) the official has no financial interest in the appointee; (2) there is no understanding between the official and 
the appointee as to how the appointee will vote; and (3) the appointee has not taken a position on the issue or otherwise 
expressed intentions as to how he or she might vote on particular issues. (See Ungar Advice Letter, No. A-93-277; 
McHugh Advice Letter, No. A-93-142.) 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 8 
8. Change in DTPP Boundaries: The City Council may consider an amendment to change 
the boundaries of the DTPP area. 
Absent specific facts that suggest that any of these decisions will affect the operation of the 
LLC's business or the potential for the sale of the property by the LLC, it would not appear that the 
decision would affect the value of the LLC. As noted above, however, Commission advice is the 
application of the law to a specific set of facts provided by the requestor. The Commission does not 
act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Thus, the 
application of these responses to future decisions may change depending on the facts surrounding 
the actual decisions. 
9. Substantive Amendments to DTPP regulations: The City Council may, in the long-term, 
consider changes to the a/lowed uses in the DTPP Area. It may also consider changes to 
the standards and guidelines for development (including, without limitation, height 
standards and parking requirements). 
As discussed in connection with number one above, regulatory changes imposed on the 
LLC's building could contribute to a change in the value of the LLC if it were compelled to comply 
with them. Additionally, if the requirements were imposed on future owners, it could affect the 
LLC's ability to sell its only asset, the building. Therefore, these decisions do appear to contribute 
to a change in the value of the LLC and the Councilmember would have a conflict of interest. 
JO. Below Market Rate Units: The City Council may consider caps, standards, and/or 
guidelines that would promote, encourage and incentivize the production of affordable 
housing within the DTPP area. 
As noted above, in the Barnhill advice letter, 
"[A]lthough it could not be determined whether typical renters of the 
Hotel could qualify for the new low to very low income housing units, the 
possibility exists that such low income housing units could pose competition to 
the Sequoia Hotel rooms that are typically rented out on a weekly or monthly 
basis .... Taking these differences into account, and looking to the applicable 
examples of decisions that might impact the value of a privately-held business 
under Regulation 18702.1 (b ), we believe a prudent person could find it 
reasonably foreseeable that all three proposed projects will contribute to a change 
in the value of the Councilmember Howard's business interest. Thus, she may not 
participate in the decisions." 
Consistent with that advice, consideration of caps, standards, and/or guidelines that would 
promote, encourage and incentivize the production of affordable housing within the DTPP area 
would appear to contribute to a change in the value of the LLC and the Councilmember would have 
a conflict of interest. 
11. Prioritizing Development in Certain Areas: City Council may consider amendments that 
would encourage development in certain portions of the DTPP area. Such amendments 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 9 
could take the form of reduced standards and/or guidelines as compared to other DTPP 
areas and establishment of specific development caps and incentives in priority areas. 
Without knowing if the area where the LLC operates is the area in question, we cannot 
advise as to whether the LLC would be materially affected. However, note that since the selection 
of one portion would be inextricably linked to the exclusion of other areas (including the area where 
the LLC's building is located, it would seem that the Councilmember would have a conflict of 
interest in all of these decisions. 
Segmentation 
Under certain circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may participate 
in other related decisions provided that the official's participation does not affect the decision in 
which he or she has a conflict of interest. Commission staff has consistently advised that an official 
may segment a decision in which the official has a conflict of interest from other decisions in which 
he or she does not have a conflict of interest to allow participation by the official in one or several 
related decisions as long as the decisions are not too interrelated to be considered separately. 
Regulation 18706 provides the rules for such "segmentation" of a governmental decision: 
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a 
financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the 
following conditions apply: 
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be 
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the 
decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is 
segmented from the other decisions; 
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is 
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the 
disqualified official's participation in any way; and 
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has 
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a 
reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official 
was disqualified. 
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably 
interrelated " when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, 
nullify, or alter the result of another decision." 
The decisions set forth above in which the Councilmember would have a conflict of interest 
could be segmented consistent with the requirements of the regulation. 


File No. 1-17-094 
Page No. 10 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
t General Counsel 
JWW:jgl

sections.question:
I. May Councilmember Howard make, participate in making, or influence upcoming 
decisions pertaining to the specified near-term and long-term amendments to Downtown Precise 
Plan ("DTPP")? 
2. Are the decisions for which the Councilmember has a conflict of interest segmentable 
from other decisions to allow the Councilmember to participate in the segmented decisions?

sections.analysis:
Conflict of Interest Law 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
(Section 87103.) Section 87103 defines the interests from which a conflict of interest may arise. 
These interests are: 
1. Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment3 worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
2. Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
An "indirect" investment or interest in real property is defined as "any investment or interest 
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public 
official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and 
dependent child_ren own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a JO-percent interest or greater." 
(Section 87103.) Because the councilmember has less than IO-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the investments and property owned by the LLC are not interests of the council member. 
3. Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial lending institution made in 
the regular course of business on terms available to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(c).) 
"Income" is defined to include any community property interest in the income of a spouse 
and a pro rata share of the income of any business entity or trust in which the official ( or his or her 
spouse) owns directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a I 0-percent or greater interest. (Section 
82030(a).) However, because the councilmember has less than I 0-percent ownership interest in the 
LLC, the clients of the company are not sources of income to Councilmember Howard. (Section 
82030(a).) 
4. Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103( d).) 
5. Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating 
[$470] or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) 
.:l "Indirect investment" is defined to include any investment or interest owned by the official's spouse. (Section 
87103.)  
6. Personal finances, meaning the financial effect of a governmental decision on the 
personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family. Regulation 18702.5 sets forth 
the materiality standard applicable to a personal financial effect, and subdivision (c) of that 
regulation provides that if a decision also affects an official's interest in a business entity or real 
property, materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, relating to business interests, or 
Regulation 18702.2, relating to real property interests, respectively. 
Councilmember Howard is a public official subject to the Act. Assuming her investment in 
the LLC is valued at $2,000 or more, she has an interest in the LLC as a business entity. (Section 
87103.) She also has an interest in the LLC as a source of income assuming she receives a 
distribution that equals or exceeds $500 in the 12 months prior to a governmental decision. (Section 
87103(c).) Thus, she would have a conflict of interest in decision that will have a reasonably 
foreseeable and material financial effect on the LLC. 
Foreseeability 
Explicitly Involved Interests: The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether 
an interest is explicitly involved in the decision. Regulation 1870 I (a) provides: 
"(a) Financial Interest Explicitly Involved: A financial effect on a financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the financial interest is a 
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or 
the official's agency. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the 
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any 
license, pennit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and 
includes any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as 
described in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l )-(6)."4 
None of the decisions explicitly involve the LLC. 
Not Explicitly Involved: In all other cases, Regulation 1870 I (b) provides that if a financial 
effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is 
reasonably foreseeable. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably 
foreseeable, however, if the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances 
not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Deemed Material 
The standard to determine materiality for a business entity is set forth in Regulation 
18702.I.5 
4 Regulation I 8702.2(a)( I )-(6) applies lo real property in which the official has a financial interest and 
therefore would nol apply here. 
3 See Regulation I 8702.3(a)(4). Where the source of income is a business entity, Regulation 18702.3 refers 
back lo the standard of applied in 18 702.1 for business entities.  
"(a) The reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental 
decision on a business entity in which an official has a financial interest identified 
in Section 87103(a) or (d) is material whenever the business entity: 
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the governmental decision will be 
made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or request for other government 
action concerning the business entity; 
"(2) Offers to make a sale of a service or a product to the official's 
agency: 
"(3) Bids on or enters into a written contract with the official's agency; 
"( 4) Is the named manufacturer in a purchase order of any product 
purchased by the official's agency or the sales provider of any products to the 
official's agency that aggregates to $1,000 or more in any 12-month period; 
"(5) Applies for a pennit, license, grant, tax credit, exception, variance, or 
other entitlement that the official's agency is authorized to issue; 
"(6) Is the subject of any inspection, action, or proceeding subject to the 
regulatory authority of the official's agency; or 
"(7) Is otherwise subject to an action taken by the official's agency, the 
effect of which is directed solely at the business entity in which the official has an 
interest." 
None of the decisions you describe fall under the tests set forth above. 
Material if Changes B11si11ess' Stock or Value 
According to Regulation 18702.1 (b ), for all other decisions, the financial effect is material if 
a prudent person with sufficient infonnation would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's publicly 
traded stock, or the value of a privately-held business entity. 
Analysis and Conclusions 
In the Barnhill Advice Letter, No. A-16-144 we analyzed a possible conflict of interest of 
Councilmember Howard's with respect to specific projects. In that letter we noted: 
"[T]he Sequoia Hotel has several retail shops such as the liquor/grocery 
store, nail salon and tobacco shop that undoubtedly rely on walk-up business. By 
adding population to the general area, each of these projects potentially adds to 
any existing customer base already enjoyed by these shops. And it is safe to 
assume that such a potential increase in retail traffic would certainly increase the 
value of the Hotel's retail leases.

decisions pertaining to the specified near-term and long-term amendments to Downtown Precise 
Plan ("DTPP")?

from other decisions to allow the Councilmember to participate in the segmented decisions?
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 10, 2017 Jonathan W. Smith Deputy City Attorney Office of the City Attorney 1017 Middlefield Road Redwood City, CA 94063 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. 1-17-094 Dear Mr. Smith: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Redwood City Councilmember Diane Howard regarding her duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"}.1 Please note that we only provide advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. We are not a finder of fact when rendering assistance (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any assistance we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Given that your questions are general in nature with limited facts, we treat your request as one for informal assistance.2 QUESTIONS I. May Councilmember Howard make, participate in making, or influence upcoming decisions pertaining to the specified near-term and long-term amendments to Downtown Precise Plan ("DTPP")? 2. Are the decisions for which the Councilmember has a conflict of interest segmentable from other decisions to allow the Councilmember to participate in the segmented decisions? FACTS The DTPP: Development in the downtown area is governed by the DTPP. The DTPP establishes land use, development, and urban design regulations for the 183-acre DTPP area. It sets: 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 Infonnal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or fonnal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation I 8329(c)(3).) File No. 1-17-094 Page No. 2 • Maximum Allowable Development caps ("MAD" Caps) of2,500 net new dwelling units; • 100,000 square feet of net new retail development; • 500,000 square feet of net new commercial office space; and • 200 net new lodging rooms. For each project, any net new units and/or square footage are subtracted from the amount available for other projects under the relevant MAD Cap. As of February 13, 2017, after accounting for alJ approved and proposed projects, the following development units are left: • 179 net new dwelling units; • 99,028 square feet of net new retail development; • 610 square feet of net new commercial office space; and • 200 net new lodging rooms remained available under the MAD Caps.
